title: Design Dropbox — File Storage & Sync
description: Design a file storage and synchronization platform. Covers blob storage architecture, chunking and delta sync, conflict resolution (versioning, multi-device merge), caching hot files at the edge, upload/download optimization (resumable, multi-part), sharing and permissions, and how SAP's DMS and ArchiveLink provide a familiar conceptual bridge.

description.value:
Functional requirements: (1) File upload/download via API, desktop client, mobile app, and web. (2) File sync across multiple devices — a change on one device propagates to all others. (3) File sharing with other users (specific files or folders). (4) Version history — access previous versions of a file (30-day or 180-day history). (5) Conflict resolution — if two users edit the same file simultaneously, both versions are saved. (6) Offline access — sync files for offline editing, sync changes on reconnect.

Non-functional requirements: (1) Durability: 99.999999999% (11 nines) — stored across multiple availability zones with erasure coding. (2) Availability: 99.99% — upload/download must always succeed. (3) Latency: first-byte read < 100ms for files < 1 MB. (4) Write throughput: support 10M+ files uploaded per day, 10TB+ of new data per day. (5) Sync latency: changes propagate to all devices within 60 seconds (eventual consistency model).

SAP Bridge: SAP DMS (Document Management System) stores files (attachments, drawings, specifications) linked to business objects (material master, sales order, equipment). The concept is the same: a file is uploaded, versioned, and linked to a logical entity. The difference is scale: SAP DMS stores millions of documents; Dropbox stores billions. The file sync mechanism (local agent, delta sync, conflict resolution) is where Dropbox's engineering challenge lies — SAP DMS doesn't have client-side sync.

The core technical challenge is: how does Dropbox know which parts of a file changed, so it can upload only the changed parts (delta sync) instead of the entire file? For a 1 GB file, re-uploading the entire file on every save is not feasible.

Fixed-size chunking (infeasible): Split the file into fixed 4 MB blocks. If the user inserts a single byte at the beginning of the file, every single block shifts by 1 byte — all blocks' hashes change, requiring a full re-upload. Fixed-size chunking cannot handle insertions/deletions efficiently.

Content-defined chunking (CDC) — the right approach: Split the file at content-defined boundaries using a sliding window. The algorithm computes a hash (Rabin fingerprint) over a small window (48 bytes). When the hash matches a predefined pattern (e.g., hash % N == 0), a chunk boundary is inserted. Since boundaries are defined by content, not position, inserting bytes shifts only the chunk that contains the insertion.

How CDC works for sync: (1) Client splits the file into chunks using CDC. (2) For each chunk, the client computes a hash (SHA-256 of the chunk content). (3) Client sends the list of chunk hashes to the server. (4) Server compares against the stored file's chunk hashes. (5) Server tells the client: 'I already have chunks 1, 3, 4 — send me chunk 2.' (6) Client uploads only the missing chunks. The server reconstructs the file by concatenating the chunks in order. This is the rsync algorithm applied to a cloud storage context.

Average chunk size tradeoff: 4 MB chunks → fewer hashes to compare (250 for a 1 GB file) but larger delta downloads. 512 KB chunks → finer granularity (2000 hashes for 1 GB) but more hash computation and comparison. Dropbox uses ~4 MB average chunk size, balancing bandwidth savings with metadata overhead.

File metadata (name, path, permissions, version history) is stored separately from file content. This separation is critical: metadata changes are frequent (renames, shares, permission changes) and must be transactional. Content changes are large but less frequent.

Metadata store (SQL database — sharded by user): Each user's file tree is stored in a relational database (InnoDB, sharded by user_id). The file tree is a representation of nodes (files and folders) with parent-child relationships. Key operations: (1) Create file — INSERT node with parent_id, name, size, mime_type. (2) Rename — UPDATE node SET name. (3) Move — UPDATE node SET parent_id. (4) Share — INSERT permission record. (5) Version history — INSERT version record on every write (stores reference to content chunk list).

Content-addressable storage: Chunks are stored in a content-addressable blob store (S3/GCS, or Dropbox's custom 'Magic Pocket'). The chunk's SHA-256 hash is its address. This provides: (1) Deduplication — if two users upload the same file, only one copy of each chunk is stored. (2) Integrity — fetching by hash guarantees the content is correct. (3) Immutability — chunks are never modified; new file versions reference new chunk lists.

Conflict resolution: When two devices sync changes to the same file concurrently: (1) The server detects the conflict (version IDs don't match). (2) The server saves both versions: the original file is renamed to 'filename (conflicted copy).' (3) Both files are synced to both devices. (4) The user sees the conflict and manually merges or chooses which version to keep. This is a 'last-write-wins with conflict copy' model — simpler than CRDT/OT because files are binary blobs (not structured text).

Resumable upload: Large file uploads are split into chunks (4 MB). Each chunk is uploaded independently with a chunk ID. If the upload is interrupted, the client resumes from the last successfully uploaded chunk. The server tracks: 'User A uploaded chunks 0-127 of file F; chunk 128 is missing.' On resume, the client uploads chunk 128 onwards. This is critical for: mobile clients switching networks, desktop clients going to sleep, or long uploads (multi-GB files).

Multi-part download (parallel chunk fetch): Large file downloads fetch chunks in parallel (e.g., 4-8 concurrent HTTP requests). Each chunk is downloaded independently and reassembled by the client. Parallel download reduces download time by 3-5x for large files on high-bandwidth connections (the bottleneck is TCP window, not bandwidth).

LAN sync (Dropbox LAN sync): Devices on the same local network discover each other via mDNS/Bonjour. Instead of downloading a file from Dropbox's servers over the internet, the device downloads from another device on the same LAN that already has the file. This reduces internet bandwidth usage by 90%+ for large files in office environments.

details: FAANG Interview Tip: The Dropbox question tests: (1) Your understanding of large-scale blob storage and content-addressed storage. (2) Your knowledge of delta sync algorithms (content-defined chunking, rsync). (3) Your ability to design a file system abstraction (tree of nodes) with transactional metadata. The SAP engineer with DMS and ArchiveLink experience has a strong foundation — the concepts of content repositories, version management, and document linking map directly. The delta sync algorithm is the new concept to emphasize (SAP doesn't do this).
